nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon reefs and sheltered embayments, almost always in association with the bubble-tip anemone Entacmaea quadricolor.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and ledges
host anemone (Entacmaea quadricolor) optional but appreciated

diet.notes: Readily accepts a varied diet of marine flake, pellet, frozen mysis, frozen brine shrimp, and herbivore preparations with marine algae. The Tomato Clownfish is generally considered an enthusiastic feeder. Two to three small feedings per day is the usual recommendation.

imageCandidates.notes: Brian Gratwicke, 2439x1737. Vivid bright tomato-red individual, tack sharp, whole fish in frame, single white head bar behind the eye clearly shown - best diagnostic display of species coloration.
Lonnie Huffman, 2592x1728. Mature adult with dark flanks and red-orange face/belly, single white bar, sharp full-body profile near an anemone - shows the darker mature-female colour phase.
Nick Hobgood, 2500x1966. In-situ within a host anemone (matches careNotes host association), sharp side profile, dark red body with bright single white head bar - natural-habitat context.

summary: The Tomato Clownfish (Amphiprion frenatus) is a bold, red-orange anemonefish from the Western Pacific, generally considered one of the hardier clownfish species available in the hobby. Adults tend to grow larger and more territorial than the common Ocellaris Clownfish, and are most readily hosted by the bubble-tip anemone.
careNotes: Tomato Clownfish are widely regarded as one of the hardiest and most beginner-friendly marine fish, and captive-bred individuals are abundantly available in the trade. Adults tend to grow larger than common ocellaris clownfish (females reach roughly 14 cm) and are generally known for becoming territorial once established, so a tank of at least around 120 L with open swimming space and rockwork territories is advised. They host most readily on the bubble-tip anemone (Entacmaea quadricolor) and occasionally on Heteractis crispa, but a host anemone is not required for long-term health. Generally considered reef-safe; isolated cases of nipping at LPS polyps near their host territory have been reported. Best kept singly or as a bonded pair; have been known to be aggressive toward other clownfish species and toward conspecifics introduced after one has established a territory.
breedingNotes: The Tomato Clownfish has been bred in captivity by aquarists and commercial facilities alike, and is generally considered one of the more straightforward clownfish to spawn. Pairs form monogamous bonds and lay demersal, adhesive eggs on a flat surface near the host anemone. The male guards and fans the clutch until hatching, typically around seven to nine days. Larvae are pelagic and require rotifers as a first food, followed by enriched Artemia nauplii.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Pomacentridae), max adult size (14 cm TL), native range (Western Pacific: Gulf of Thailand to Palau, Japan to Java), depth range (1-12 m), wild temperature range (25.4-29.1 C), and protandrous hermaphrodite biology.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used as taxonomy/biology fallback after primary LiveAquaria URL initially 404'd; confirmed 14 cm max size, host anemones Entacmaea quadricolor and Heteractis crispa, sequential (protandrous) hermaphroditism, and aggression in mature adults.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs spawn readily in captivity on flat surfaces adjacent to a host anemone or rock. The male tends the clutch until hatching, after which larvae require rotifer culture as a first food.
